#Summary1=(human1) The author argues the superiority of public over private systems, as privatization prioritizes profits over providing good services, leading to costly and inferior systems such as private health care in the U.S., in contrast to public services such as the USPS, which are designed to serve as many people as possible for the lowest price possible.
#Text=1. The Profit Motive Moves Most of the Money to the Top
#Text=The federal Medicare Administrator made $ 170,000 in 2010 .
#Text=The president of MD Anderson Cancer Center in Texas made over ten times as much in 2012 .
#Text=Stephen J. Hemsley , the CEO of United Health Group , made almost 300 times as much in one year , $ 48 million , most of it from company stock .
#Text=In part because of such inequities in compensation , our private health care system is the most expensive system in the developed world .
#Text=The price of common surgeries is anywhere from three to ten times higher in the U.S. than in Great Britain , Canada , France , or Germany .

#Text=Two of the documented examples : an $ 8,000 special stress test for which Medicare would have paid $ 554 ; and a $ 60,000 gall bladder operation , for which a private insurance company was willing to pay $ 2,000 .
#Text=Medicare , on the other hand , which is largely without the profit motive and the competing sources of billing , is efficiently run , for all eligible Americans .
#Text=According to the Council for Affordable Health Insurance and other sources , medical administrative costs are much higher for private insurance than for Medicare .
#Text=But the privatizers keep encroaching on the public sector .
#Text=Our government reimburses the CEOs of private contractors at a rate approximately double what we pay the President .
#Text=Overall , we pay the corporate bosses over $ 7 billion a year .
#Text=Many Americans do n't realize that the privatization of Social Security and Medicare would transfer much of our money to yet another group of CEOs .
#Text=2. Privatization Serves People with Money , the Public Sector Serves Everyone
#Text=A good example is the U.S. Postal Service ( USPS ) , which is legally required to serve every home in the country .
#Text=Fedex and United Parcel Service ( UPS ) ca n't serve unprofitable locations .
#Text=Yet the USPS is much cheaper for small packages .
#Text=An online comparison revealed the following for the two - day shipment of a similarly - sized envelope to another state :
#Text=– USPS 2 - Day $ 5.68 ( 46 cents without the 2 - day restriction )
#Text=– FedEx 2 - Day $ 19.28
#Text=– UPS , 2 Day $ 24.09
#Text=USPS is so inexpensive , in fact , that Fedex actually uses the U.S. Post Office for about 30 percent of its ground shipments .
#Text=Another example is education .
#Text=A recent ProPublica report found that in the past twenty years four - year state colleges have been serving a diminishing portion of the country 's lowest - income students .
#Text=At the K - 12 level , cost - saving business strategies apply to the privatization of our children 's education .
#Text=Charter schools are less likely to accept students with disabilities .
#Text=Charter teachers have fewer years of experience and a higher turnover rate .
#Text=Non-teacher positions have insufficient retirement plans and health insurance , and much lower pay .
#Text=Finally , with regard to health care , 43 percent of sick Americans skipped doctor 's visits and / or medication purchases in 2011 because of excessive costs .
#Text=It 's estimated that over 40,000 Americans die every year because they ca n't afford health insurance .
#Text=3. Privatization Turns Essential Human Needs into Products
#Text=Big business would like to privatize our water .

#Text=A Citigroup economist exulted , " Water as an asset class will , in my view , become eventually the single most important physical - commodity based asset class , dwarfing oil , copper , agricultural commodities and precious metals . "
#Text=They want our federal land .
#Text=Attempts at privatization were made by the Reagan administration in the 1980s and the Republican - controlled Congress in the 1990s .
#Text=In 2006 , President Bush proposed auctioning off 300,000 acres of national forest in 41 states .

#Text=Paul Ryan 's Path to Prosperity was based in part on Republican Jason Chaffetz ' " Disposal of Excess Federal Lands Act of 2011 , " which would unload millions of acres of land in America 's west .
#Text=They want our cities .
#Text=A privatization expert told the Detroit Free Press that the real money is in urban assets with a " revenue stream . "
#Text=So Detroit 's most valuable resource , its Water & Sewerage Department ( DWSD ) , is the collateral for a loan of $ 350 million to pay off the banks handling the litigation .
#Text=Bloomberg estimates a cost of almost half a billion dollars , in a city where homeowners can barely afford the water services .
#Text=And they want our bodies .
#Text=One - fifth of the human genome is privately owned through patents .
#Text=Strains of influenza and hepatitis have been claimed by corporate and university labs , and because of this researchers ca n't use the patented life forms to perform cancer research .
#Text=4. Public Systems Promote a Strong Middle Class
#Text=Part of free - market mythology is that public employees and union workers are greedy takers , enjoying benefits that average private sector workers are denied .
#Text=But the facts show that government and union workers are not overpaid .
#Text=According to the Census Bureau , state and local government employees make up 14.5 % of the U.S. workforce and receive 14.3 % of the total compensation .
#Text=Union members make up about 12 % of the workforce , but their total pay amounts to just 10 % of adjusted gross income as reported to the IRS .
#Text=The average private sector worker makes about the same salary as a state or local government worker .
#Text=But the median salary for U.S. workers , 83 % of whom are in the private sector , was $ 18,000 less in 2009 , at $ 26,261 .
#Text=Inequality is much more pervasive in the private sector .
